_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AAOS clinical guidelines, American Academy of Neurology carpal tunnel guidelines, NICE NG185, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) is the most common peripheral nerve entrapment disorder, resulting from compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel — a narrow, rigid space on the palm-side of the wrist formed by the carpal bones and the transverse carpal ligament. The median nerve provides sensation to the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and half of the ring finger, and motor function to the thumb's thenar muscles. CTS is characterized by pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness in the median nerve distribution of the hand, typically worse at night and with repetitive or forceful hand use. The condition affects 3-6% of the general adult population, with an estimated annual economic burden exceeding $2 billion in the United States alone due to lost productivity and medical costs. It is particularly common in occupations involving repetitive wrist flexion and gripping — data entry workers, assembly line workers, and musicians carry significantly elevated risk. Diagnosis is clinical, confirmed by nerve conduction studies. Treatment ranges from conservative management (wrist splinting, activity modification, corticosteroid injection) for mild-to-moderate cases, to surgical release of the transverse carpal ligament for moderate-to-severe cases and those failing conservative care — a procedure that produces excellent outcomes in the majority of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 3-6% of the general adult population; lifetime risk estimated at 10% in the general population
keyStats.demographics: Women are 3 times more likely than men; peak onset in the 45-60 age group; 50% of occupational CTS cases resolve within 10 months of onset
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed in adults aged 45-60; occupational cases peak in the 40s-50s; rare in children
keyStats.globalCases: Most common peripheral nerve entrapment; 275,000-500,000 carpal tunnel releases performed annually in the United States

definition: Carpal tunnel syndrome (ICD-10: G56.0 for carpal tunnel syndrome; G56.00 bilateral, G56.01 right, G56.02 left) is a compressive neuropathy of the median nerve at the wrist. The carpal tunnel is formed by the concave arch of the carpal bones (navicular, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform on the medial side; trapezoid, capitate, hamate, scaphoid on the lateral side) on three sides, and the transverse carpal ligament (TCL — a strong, inelastic fibrous band) spanning across the top on the palm side. Through this unyielding space pass nine flexor tendons (to the fingers) and the median nerve. Any process that reduces the available space within the carpal tunnel — tendon sheath swelling, edema, bone spurs, tumor, or congenital small carpal tunnel — increases pressure on the median nerve. Sustained pressure above 20-30 mmHg impairs median nerve axonal transport, causing demyelination (earliest change), and eventually axonal loss (in severe cases). The nerve becomes ischemic distal to the compression site, leading to the characteristic numbness, tingling, and eventually thenar muscle weakness and atrophy.

typesClassification.description: Intermittent paresthesia (numbness/tingling) in median nerve distribution, occurring with repetitive hand use or at night. No objective sensory loss on examination; normal nerve conduction studies or mildly prolonged distal sensory latency. Responds well to conservative treatment — wrist splinting and activity modification.

Frequent paresthesia, often present during the day, with some sensory blunting on examination (two-point discrimination >6 mm). Nerve conduction studies show prolonged distal sensory latency and/or motor latency. May respond to conservative treatment but often requires corticosteroid injection or surgical release.

Constant paresthesia, significant sensory loss in median nerve distribution, and/or thenar muscle weakness (grip weakness, thumb opposition difficulty) and atrophy (visible wasting of the thenar eminence). Nerve conduction studies show elevated latencies and reduced amplitude of median nerve action potentials. Surgical release is the treatment of choice.

Involvement of both hands, present in approximately 50% of CTS patients. When bilateral, always warrants evaluation for systemic causes — diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, pregnancy, and amyloidosis are all associated with bilateral CTS.

CTS developing as a result of repetitive hand-intensive occupational tasks. While occupational risk factors are well-established, the relationship is more complex than once thought — high-force, high-repetition, and vibrating tool use are the most significant occupational risk factors. Ergonomic interventions, task rotation, and early ergonomic assessment reduce risk.

primarySymptoms: Numbness and tingling in the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and half of the ring finger closest to the thumb (median nerve distribution) — the hallmark symptom. The little finger is NOT affected, which helps distinguish CTS from other causes of hand numbness.
Pain or burning in the hand, particularly in the palm and fingers, radiating up the forearm toward the elbow — caused by median nerve ischemia.

Symptoms worse at night — patients often wake from sleep with numb, tingling hands, and find relief by shaking the hand ('flick sign'). Sleeping with the wrist in flexion (bent forward) increases carpal tunnel pressure and worsens symptoms.
Weakness in grip strength and pinch — patients drop objects, have difficulty opening jars, and notice reduced thumb-to-index finger pinch strength. In severe cases, thenar muscle atrophy is visible.

Symptoms triggered or worsened by repetitive hand use — typing, gripping tools, driving, holding a phone — activities that involve sustained wrist flexion or extension increase carpal tunnel pressure.
Loss of fine motor coordination — difficulty with tasks requiring precise thumb-index finger opposition (buttoning, writing, picking up small objects) due to median nerve motor dysfunction.
Symptoms may alternate between hands — bilateral CTS often presents with alternating symptoms, one hand worse than the other at any given time.
earlyWarningSigns: Numbness or tingling in the thumb and first two fingers — particularly first noticed at night or when driving
Waking with numb, tingling hands that improve after shaking the hands — the 'flick sign' is highly suggestive of CTS
Difficulty with fine motor tasks — buttoning a shirt, writing, using chopsticks — that was not previously difficult
Reduced grip strength — noticing that jars are harder to open, or objects are being dropped more frequently
Hand pain or numbness after prolonged typing, data entry, or repetitive hand work
Aching in the forearm or wrist after hand-intensive work
emergencySigns: Sudden complete loss of sensation and motor function in the hand — may indicate acute median nerve compression from hemorrhage, fracture, or infection requiring urgent surgical decompression
Severe, unrelenting pain unresponsive to ordinary measures — particularly if accompanied by swelling, redness, and warmth — may indicate infection or inflammatory arthritis

Signs of acute compartment syndrome of the forearm — severe pain with passive finger stretch, tenseness of the forearm compartment, pain out of proportion to examination, and neurological deficit — a surgical emergency
Fracture of the distal radius or carpal bones with subsequent hand numbness — requires urgent orthopedic evaluation
Associated fever, chills, and severe hand pain with limited motion — may indicate septic arthritis requiring urgent care

causes.cause: Repetitive hand use and forceful gripping
causes.description: The most common occupational risk factors — repetitive wrist movements (especially flexion and extension), forceful gripping, and exposure to vibration cause swelling of the flexor tendon sheaths within the carpal tunnel, reducing available space and increasing pressure on the median nerve. Data entry workers, assembly line workers, meat packers, musicians, and construction workers are at elevated risk.

Pregnancy-associated CTS affects 25-60% of pregnant women, particularly in the third trimester. Fluid retention and hormonal changes increase edema within the carpal tunnel. Most cases resolve within weeks to months postpartum. Oral contraceptive use and menstruation-related fluctuations can cause similar symptoms.

causes.cause: Medical conditions causing generalized edema or tissue swelling
causes.description: Hypothyroidism (myxedema causes tissue swelling), diabetes mellitus (impaired nerve microcirculation and mild tissue edema), rheumatoid arthritis (synovial inflammation of the flexor tendons), renal failure (fluid retention), and congestive heart failure all increase carpal tunnel pressure or reduce nerve tolerance to compression.

causes.cause: Anatomical factors — congenital small carpal tunnel
causes.description: Some individuals are born with a structurally smaller-than-average carpal tunnel, predisposing to nerve compression with even minor increases in carpal tunnel contents. This explains why some people develop CTS in their 20s and 30s without obvious precipitating factors.

Fractures of the distal radius, scaphoid, or other carpal bones — particularly those that heal with displacement or angulation — alter carpal tunnel anatomy and increase median nerve compression risk. Post-fracture CTS may develop acutely (nerve contusion) or years later (carpal tunnel narrowing from malunion or bone spurs).

causes.cause: Space-occupying lesions within the carpal tunnel
causes.description: Ganglion cysts, lipomas, hemangiomas, synovial cysts, accessory muscles, and rarely tumors (schwannomas of the median nerve) occupy space within the carpal tunnel and cause compression. These are uncommon causes but should be considered when CTS is unilateral, progressive, and atypical.

Rheumatoid arthritis, gout (urate crystal deposition in the tendon sheaths), amyloidosis, and systemic lupus erythematosus cause inflammatory swelling within the carpal tunnel that compresses the median nerve.

riskFactors.description: Women are 3 times more likely to develop CTS than men. Contributing factors include smaller carpal tunnel dimensions relative to the median nerve, hormonal fluctuations (pregnancy, menopause), and higher likelihood of being in repetitive hand-use occupations.
Peak onset in middle age. The cumulative effect of years of repetitive hand use, age-related tissue changes, and higher prevalence of comorbidities at this age explains the peak.

Occupations requiring sustained repetitive wrist flexion or extension, high-force gripping, and vibration exposure (meat cutting, assembly line, masonry, computer work) carry significantly elevated risk. Ergonomic interventions and task rotation reduce occupational risk.

Diabetic patients have a 2-4 fold increased risk of CTS. Both microvascular disease (impaired nerve perfusion) and increased tissue glycosylation (making tissues less compliant within the carpal tunnel) contribute.
Clinical and subclinical hypothyroidism are associated with 2-3 fold increased CTS risk due to myxedema (tissue glycosaminoglycan accumulation) causing swelling within the carpal tunnel.
25-60% of pregnant women develop CTS symptoms, particularly in the third trimester. Fluid retention and hormonal changes are the mechanism. Most cases resolve within 3 months postpartum.

A history of distal radius, carpal bone, or forearm fracture — even if healed — is associated with moderately elevated CTS risk due to altered carpal tunnel anatomy from malunion, bone spurs, or post-traumatic fibrosis.

Elevated BMI is a consistent risk factor for CTS. Mechanism: increased systemic inflammation, higher carpal tunnel pressure at baseline, and higher rates of associated conditions (diabetes, hypothyroidism) in obese individuals.

affectedDemographics: Carpal tunnel syndrome affects 3-6% of the general adult population, with a lifetime risk of approximately 10%
Women are affected 3 times more frequently than men; the gender gap is most pronounced in the 45-60 age group
The highest incidence occurs in the 45-60 age group; occupational CTS peaks in the 40s-50s
Approximately 50% of cases resolve within 10 months of onset without treatment; 50% persist beyond this
Occupational CTS accounts for approximately 50% of all CTS cases in occupational settings; assembly workers, meat packers, and data entry workers have the highest rates
Bilateral CTS occurs in approximately 50% of patients; unilateral cases are more common in non-diabetics
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of carpal tunnel syndrome is primarily clinical, based on a history of median nerve distribution symptoms (numbness, tingling, pain) with characteristic aggravating factors (night symptoms, repetitive hand use) and examination findings of median nerve dysfunction. Physical examination includes: Tinel's sign (tapping over the carpal tunnel reproduces paresthesia in median nerve distribution — present in 50-70% of CTS patients), Phalen's test (wrist flexion for 60 seconds reproduces paresthesia — sensitivity 60-70%), and sensory examination (blunting of sensation in median nerve distribution). Thenar muscle strength and presence of atrophy are assessed. Nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG) are the gold standard for confirming diagnosis, determining severity, and ruling out other neuropathies (cervical radiculopathy, brachial plexopathy, polyneuropathy). NCS shows prolonged distal sensory and motor latencies of the median nerve across the wrist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone of diagnosis. Characteristic history: median nerve distribution paresthesia, worse at night, triggered by repetitive hand use. Key physical exam findings: Tinel's sign (percussion of the carpal tunnel produces median nerve paresthesia), Phalen's test (60 seconds of wrist flexion reproduces symptoms), and sensory blunting in median nerve distribution. Thenar muscle testing for strength and presence of atrophy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Your doctor will tap on your wrist, hold your wrist in flexion, test sensation in your fingers, and assess grip and pinch strength. Tests are briefly uncomfortable but not painful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for confirming CTS, determining severity, and excluding other causes of hand numbness (cervical radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy, brachial plexopathy). NCS measures how fast and how well the median nerve conducts signals — in CTS, distal sensory latency (>3.5 ms is abnormal) and distal motor latency (>4.5 ms is abnormal) are prolonged across the wrist. EMG assesses whether axonal loss has occurred.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A neurologist or physiatrist performs the test. Small surface electrodes are placed on the hand and forearm; a mild electrical stimulus stimulates the median nerve. You feel a tapping or tingling sensation. The test takes 30-45 minutes. Mildly uncomfortable but generally well-tolerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A non-invasive imaging test that can demonstrate median nerve swelling (cross-sectional area >10-12 mm² at the level of the pisiform is diagnostic), median nerve bowing, and space-occupying lesions within the carpal tunnel. Increasingly used as a first-line confirmatory test, particularly when MRI is not available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld ultrasound probe is moved over the wrist with acoustic gel. Non-invasive, no radiation. Takes 15-20 minutes. Results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Ordered when an anatomical cause for CTS is suspected (ganglion cyst, lipoma, anomalous muscle, bone spur) or when surgical planning requires detailed soft tissue anatomy. MRI also detects denervation changes in thenar muscles in severe cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie inside the MRI scanner with your arm extended. Takes 30-40 minutes. No radiation. The machine is loud; earplugs are provided.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (TSH, free T4, fasting glucose, HbA1c, inflammatory markers)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Ordered when bilateral CTS, recurrent CTS, or CTS in a young patient suggests an underlying systemic cause. Hypothyroidism, diabetes, and inflammatory arthritis are the most common systemic associations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results typically available within 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of carpal tunnel syndrome is individualized based on severity. Mild CTS: wrist splinting in neutral position (the most important first-line intervention), activity modification, and NSAIDs for pain. Moderate CTS: addition of corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel — a very effective procedure providing relief within days lasting weeks to months. Severe CTS with thenar muscle weakness or atrophy: surgical release is the treatment of choice regardless of trial of conservative measures. The decision to proceed to surgery is based on symptom severity, nerve study findings, and functional impairment. Surgical release of the transverse carpal ligament (carpal tunnel release) is performed open or endoscopically — both techniques effectively decompress the median nerve with 70-85% good-to-excellent outcomes. Post-surgical rehabilitation focuses on nerve gliding exercises and gradual return to activity. Without treatment, CTS follows a waxing-and-waning course — approximately 50% of patients improve spontaneously within 10 months; the remainder have progressive worsening of symptoms.

medicalTreatments.name: Wrist splinting in neutral position (night splint)
medicalTreatments.description: The single most important first-line treatment for CTS. A prefabricated or custom-molded wrist splint holds the wrist in neutral position (0 degrees of flexion and extension), which minimizes carpal tunnel pressure. Night splinting alone (worn during sleep) resolves symptoms in 30-40% of patients with mild-to-moderate CTS. Worn continuously for 4-6 weeks, it is more effective than intermittent use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: First-line treatment. 30-70% of patients with mild CTS report meaningful improvement with splinting alone. Most effective when combined with activity modification.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel
medicalTreatments.description: Injection of triamcinolone or methylprednisolone into the carpal tunnel, dorsal to the median nerve (avoiding the nerve itself), reduces inflammation and tendon sheath swelling, lowering carpal tunnel pressure. A very effective procedure for moderate CTS — provides relief within days that may last months. May be repeated once if symptoms recur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 70-90% short-to-medium term pain relief. NCS sometimes show improvement in latencies. The effect may be temporary (weeks to months); repeated injections are less effective due to tissue atrophy and carry risks including median nerve injury.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs and pain management
medicalTreatments.description: Oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) for pain control. Topical diclofenac gel applied to the volar wrist is effective for localized pain with minimal systemic side effects. Used as adjuncts to splinting and injection — they manage symptoms but do not address the underlying compression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for short-term pain relief. Not a primary treatment for the nerve compression itself.

medicalTreatments.name: Nerve gliding exercises (median nerve glides)
medicalTreatments.description: A set of specific exercises in which the wrist, fingers, and elbow are moved through a graduated range of positions designed to glide the median nerve within its sheath, reducing adherence and improving axonal transport. Taught by a hand therapist or physical therapist. Most effective in mild-to-moderate CTS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence from multiple RCTs supports nerve gliding exercises as a beneficial adjunct to splinting, particularly for mild-to-moderate CTS.

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification
medicalTreatments.description: Modifying occupational or recreational hand use to reduce repetitive wrist flexion, forceful gripping, and vibration exposure. Task rotation (alternating tasks every 20-30 minutes), ergonomic keyboard/mouse positioning, ergonomic tools with reduced grip force requirements, and anti-vibration gloves reduce mechanical stress on the carpal tunnel.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with splinting, activity modification reduces symptoms in occupational CTS.

surgicalOptions.name: Open carpal tunnel release
surgicalOptions.description: The gold standard surgical procedure. A small incision (2-4 cm) is made in the palm at the base of the palm, directly over the transverse carpal ligament. The TCL is completely divided (released), decompressing the median nerve. The wound is closed and dressed. The procedure takes 10-20 minutes under local anesthesia with sedation or regional block. Takes 2-3 weeks for light activities and 4-6 weeks for heavy activities.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% good-to-excellent outcomes for symptom relief. NCS improvement takes 3-6 months. Outcomes are less favorable when there is severe axonal loss (significant pre-operative sensory loss, thenar atrophy), severe diabetes, or worker's compensation involvement.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic carpal tunnel release (single or dual portal)
surgicalOptions.description: A minimally invasive technique using an endoscope (small camera) introduced through a small incision or portal to visualize and cut the transverse carpal ligament from inside the carpal tunnel. Two approaches: single portal (Sisko method — one incision at the wrist flexion crease) and dual portal (Agee method — one incision at the wrist, one in the palm). Supposed advantage is less postoperative palm pain and faster return to work, but long-term outcomes are equivalent to open release.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent long-term outcomes to open release (70-85% good-to-excellent). May have faster early recovery and less palm tenderness at 6-12 weeks. The difference in long-term outcomes between open and endoscopic is minimal.

surgicalOptions.name: Revision carpal tunnel release
surgicalOptions.description: A repeat surgery for patients whose CTS has recurred after an initial release. Required in approximately 5-10% of surgical cases. The scar tissue around the median nerve is removed (neurolysis), the ligament is re-released if incomplete, and the nerve is mobilized. Outcomes are less favorable than primary release — 50-70% improvement in symptom relief.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 50-70% good-to-excellent outcomes in recurrent CTS — lower than primary release due to scar tissue, incomplete initial release, and more severe nerve damage at the time of revision.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hand therapy and median nerve gliding exercises
alternativeTreatments.description: A hand therapist (occupational or physical therapist with hand specialization) provides structured nerve gliding exercises, tendon gliding exercises (to reduce flexor tendon adhesion), and ergonomic education. The combination of splinting plus hand therapy is more effective than either alone.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Multiple RCTs support the combination of splinting and nerve gliding exercises for mild-to-moderate CTS.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Acupuncture targeting the wrist, hand, and arm may provide modest short-term symptom relief for CTS. Low-quality evidence suggests benefit in mild cases.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Low-quality evidence for modest short-term benefit in mild CTS. Not recommended as primary treatment for moderate-to-severe CTS.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitamin B6 deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy-like symptoms, and B6 supplementation has been studied in CTS with mixed results. Some studies show modest benefit; most show no significant advantage over placebo.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Inconsistent evidence — some small studies show modest benefit in mild CTS, others show no benefit. Not a primary treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Therapeutic ultrasound
alternativeTreatments.description: A non-invasive modality using ultrasound waves to heat tissues within the carpal tunnel, potentially reducing inflammation and improving nerve gliding. Evidence is limited.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Insufficient evidence to support routine use.

whySeeSpecialist: A hand surgeon (orthopedic or plastic surgeon with hand fellowship) or neurosurgeon specializing in peripheral nerve should be seen when: CTS is moderate to severe (constant symptoms, sensory loss, grip weakness); NCS show severe nerve compression (high latencies, reduced amplitude); there is thenar muscle weakness or atrophy; conservative treatment (splinting, injection) has failed after 6-12 weeks; symptoms are bilateral (to screen for systemic causes); or CTS is suspected in a diabetic patient with moderate-to-severe nerve disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified hand surgeon — either an orthopedic surgeon or plastic surgeon who has completed a hand and upper extremity fellowship (CAQSH — Certificate of Added Qualification in Surgery of the Hand). Ask about their annual volume of carpal tunnel releases. A hand therapist (occupational or physical therapist with a CHT — Certified Hand Therapist credential) is the primary clinician for conservative management. Continuity matters — CTS treatment requires follow-up to assess response to splinting and injection and to make timely decisions about surgical escalation.
preventionStrategies: Maintain neutral wrist posture during hand-intensive tasks — avoid sustained wrist flexion, extension, or deviation; use ergonomic tools and keyboards that allow neutral wrist positioning
Take frequent breaks — the American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists recommends task interruptions every 20-30 minutes during repetitive hand work
Use ergonomic tools with reduced grip force requirements — ergonomic studies consistently show that reducing grip force reduces carpal tunnel pressure
Avoid vibration exposure — anti-vibration gloves and tool design reduce vibration-related CTS risk in occupational settings
Maintain healthy body weight — obesity is a consistent risk factor for CTS
Treat underlying medical conditions — optimal management of diabetes, hypothyroidism, and rheumatoid arthritis reduces CTS risk and severity
lifestyleModifications: Wear a wrist splint at night — sleeping with the wrist in flexion worsens CTS; a night splint maintains neutral position during sleep
Use a padded wrist rest with keyboard and mouse — keep the wrist in a neutral posture during computer use
Modify hand tools and sports equipment — use ergonomically designed tools, racquet grips, and gardening gloves that reduce grip force
Avoid prolonged phone use in the hand — use speakerphone, headsets, or the other hand
Apply ice to the wrist after hand-intensive activities if the wrist feels swollen or painful
Elevate the hand when possible — reducing wrist edema helps lower carpal tunnel pressure

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet — Mediterranean-style diet rich in omega-3 fatty fish, olive oil, vegetables, and nuts reduces systemic inflammation that contributes to tissue swelling within the carpal tunnel
Vitamin B6 adequate intake — vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) is essential for peripheral nerve function; deficiency can worsen CTS symptoms; foods include poultry, fish, potatoes, bananas, and chickpeas
Water — well-hydrated connective tissue is more extensible and less prone to inflammation
Adequate magnesium — magnesium supports nerve function and muscle relaxation; foods include dark chocolate, avocados, nuts, and leafy greens
avoid: Excessive sodium — high sodium intake promotes fluid retention and wrist edema that increases carpal tunnel pressure
Processed foods and refined carbohydrates — these promote systemic inflammation
Alcohol — excessive alcohol worsens peripheral neuropathy and impairs nerve healing
Smoking — smoking causes vasoconstriction and reduces blood flow to peripheral nerves, potentially worsening CTS

exerciseGuidelines: The primary evidence-based exercises for CTS are nerve gliding (median nerve glides) and tendon gliding exercises. Median nerve glides: (1) Start with wrist neutral, fingers and thumb flexed. (2) Extend fingers and thumb. (3) Extend wrist while keeping fingers flexed. (4) Extend wrist and fingers while extending elbow (arm straight). (5) Add shoulder abduction and extension. Hold each position for 3-5 seconds; repeat 5-10 times, 2-3 times daily. Tendon gliding: make a fist, then fully extend fingers (fist-to-fan stretch), repeating 5-10 times, 2-3 times daily. Stretching exercises should NOT cause increased numbness or pain — if they do, reduce intensity. Application of heat before exercises improves nerve gliding; application of ice after exercises reduces inflammation.
dailyManagement: Wear wrist splint every night — this is the single most important daily management step for CTS
Perform median nerve glides and tendon glides twice daily — consistency is key for maintaining nerve mobility
Modify or avoid activities that trigger symptoms — typically sustained wrist flexion, forceful gripping, and vibrating tools
Apply ice to the wrist after activities that aggravate symptoms
Use ergonomic positioning at workstations — keyboard at elbow height or slightly below, wrists floating (not resting on desk)
Track symptoms — note which activities trigger symptoms and how long symptoms persist after the activity; this helps identify modifiable triggers
prognosis: The natural history of untreated CTS is variable — approximately 50% of patients improve spontaneously within 10 months without specific treatment; the other 50% have persistent or progressive symptoms. Severe sensory loss, thenar muscle weakness, and atrophy at presentation are poor prognostic signs — these indicate established axonal loss, and even surgical decompression may not restore full function if the nerve has been compressed for too long. Age over 50, duration of symptoms >12 months before treatment, severe NCS abnormalities (markedly prolonged latencies, reduced amplitudes), and diabetes are all associated with poorer surgical outcomes. With appropriate treatment, approximately 70-85% of surgically treated patients achieve satisfactory relief.
recoveryTimeline: Non-operative treatment: initial improvement with wrist splinting within 2-4 weeks for mild cases; full effect at 6-12 weeks. Corticosteroid injection: relief within 3-7 days, lasting 4 weeks to 6 months depending on severity; the effect may be sustained or may require repeat injection. Surgical release: immediate decompression — most patients note relief of night pain within 24-48 hours of surgery; return to light activities (driving, light office work) at 2-3 weeks; full return to heavy labor and grip-intensive activities at 6-8 weeks. Nerve function (sensory and motor) may continue to improve for 6-12 months after surgery as the nerve remodels.
complications: Permanent median nerve damage — when CTS with axonal loss is left untreated for too long, the nerve damage becomes irreversible. Thenar muscle atrophy that has been present for more than 12-18 months may not recover even after surgical decompression.

Recurrent or persistent symptoms after surgery — approximately 5-15% of patients have inadequate symptom relief or symptom recurrence after carpal tunnel release; causes include incomplete ligament release, scar tissue formation around the nerve, pillar pain, and incorrect initial diagnosis

Pillar pain (palmar pain) — pain at the base of the palm over the surgical site, present for weeks to months after open carpal tunnel release; more common with open than endoscopic release; usually self-limited

Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) — a rare but serious complication of carpal tunnel surgery in which the hand becomes swollen, stiff, hypersensitive, and discolored; requires urgent treatment with sympathetic nerve blocks and physical therapy
Nerve injury during injection or surgery — rare but potentially devastating complication; median nerve laceration or injury during injection can cause permanent worsening of symptoms
Scar sensitivity and hypertrophic scarring — some patients develop sensitive, raised scars at the palm incision that are bothersome during grip activities for months after surgery
Incomplete symptom resolution — even with technically successful surgery, some patients have residual symptoms due to pre-existing axonal damage that cannot be reversed by decompression alone

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Patient Education
supportResources.description: AAOS patient education resource on carpal tunnel syndrome — symptoms, risk factors, treatment options, and prevention.

supportResources.name: American Association of Neuromuscular & Electrodiagnostic Medicine (AANEM) — CTS Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Professional organization providing information on nerve conduction studies and EMG diagnosis for CTS.

supportResources.name: NICE NG185: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2020.
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline on the diagnosis and management of carpal tunnel syndrome.

supportResources.name: American Society for Surgery of the Hand (ASSH) — Patient Education
supportResources.description: ASSH patient education materials on carpal tunnel syndrome, written by hand surgeons.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical Radiculopathy (C6-C7)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervical radiculopathy causes neck pain radiating down the arm, with dermatomal (nerve root) sensory loss that extends to the entire finger (not just median nerve distribution), and often includes weakness in C6/C7 myotome muscles (deltoid, biceps). NCS/EMG distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thoracic outlet syndrome involves compression of the brachial plexus (not the median nerve) and/or subclavian vessels between the clavicle and first rib. Causes arm pain, numbness, and sometimes hand ischemia or venous congestion. The patterns of weakness and sensory loss differ from CTS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral Neuropathy (diabetic or idiopathic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral neuropathy causes diffuse, symmetric sensory loss in the feet and hands ('stocking-glove' distribution) that is not limited to the median nerve. Both hands and feet are affected simultaneously. Nerve conduction studies show a generalized polyneuropathy pattern, not focal median nerve slowing.

confusedWithConditions.name: De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: De Quervain's causes pain at the radial (thumb-side) wrist and first dorsal compartment of the wrist (abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons), not median nerve compression symptoms. The pain is localized to the radial wrist, not diffuse palmar wrist pain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ulnar Nerve Entrapment at the Elbow (Cubital Tunnel Syndrome)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ulnar nerve entrapment causes numbness in the ring and little fingers (not the thumb and first two fingers), weakness of intrinsic hand muscles (grip weakness, claw hand), and pain at the medial elbow. Does not cause thenar weakness or night symptoms characteristic of CTS.

relatedConditions.name: De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions cause wrist pain and hand symptoms and are common in occupations requiring repetitive hand use. They can coexist. De Quervain's causes pain on the thumb-side of the wrist; CTS causes numbness/tingling in the thumb-index-middle finger distribution.

relatedConditions.name: Ulnar Nerve Entrapment at the Elbow (Cubital Tunnel Syndrome)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are peripheral nerve entrapment syndromes in the upper extremity. Cubital tunnel syndrome affects the ulnar nerve at the elbow, causing numbness in the ring and little fingers and intrinsic hand weakness. CTS affects the median nerve at the wrist. They can coexist — double crush syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Cervical Radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: May coexist with CTS (double crush syndrome — compression at two levels of the same nerve). Both cause hand symptoms; the distributions, associated symptoms (neck pain in radiculopathy), and exam findings differ. NCS/EMG distinguishes both.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes is a major risk factor for CTS and worsens outcomes. Diabetic CTS is more severe, more likely bilateral, and less responsive to treatment. Optimal glycemic control improves CTS outcomes.

faqs.question: What does carpal tunnel syndrome feel like?
faqs.answer: The most characteristic symptom is numbness and tingling in the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and half of the ring finger closest to the thumb — always the thumb and first two fingers, never the little finger. Patients describe it as 'pins and needles,' a 'prickling' sensation, or the hand 'going to sleep.' The pain is often described as burning or aching in the palm, sometimes radiating up the forearm. Symptoms are classically worse at night — many patients wake from sleep with completely numb hands. Shaking the hand often provides relief (the 'flick sign'). Weakness in grip and pinch develops as the condition progresses, with dropping of objects and difficulty with fine motor tasks.

faqs.question: How is carpal tunnel syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a clinical evaluation by your doctor — they will ask about your symptoms, perform physical examination including Tinel's sign (tapping the wrist to reproduce the numbness) and Phalen's test (holding your wrist flexed for 60 seconds to reproduce symptoms), and assess grip and pinch strength. If CTS is suspected, nerve conduction studies (NCS) and electromyography (EMG) are ordered to confirm the diagnosis, determine severity, and exclude other causes of hand numbness (cervical radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy). NCS measures how quickly electrical signals travel through the median nerve — in CTS, the signals slow down as they pass through the wrist.

faqs.question: Is carpal tunnel surgery dangerous?
faqs.answer: Carpal tunnel release is one of the most common and safest surgical procedures — approximately 275,000-500,000 are performed annually in the United States alone. Major complications (nerve injury, infection, wound healing problems) occur in less than 1-2% of cases in experienced hands. The most common complication is pillar pain — tenderness at the base of the palm over the incision that can last for weeks to months after surgery. Rare but serious complications include complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS), a painful hypersensitivity state, and incomplete relief if the diagnosis was incorrect or if the nerve damage was too advanced before surgery to reverse. When performed by an experienced hand surgeon, the procedure is highly safe and effective.

faqs.question: Can carpal tunnel syndrome come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence of CTS after a properly performed carpal tunnel release is uncommon — approximately 5-15% of patients experience incomplete relief or symptom recurrence. Causes include: incomplete release of the transverse carpal ligament (the most common cause of persistent symptoms), scar tissue forming around the median nerve (neurolysis), a second space-occupying lesion in the carpal tunnel (ganglion cyst, lipoma), or incorrect initial diagnosis (symptoms actually due to cervical radiculopathy or neuropathy). Revision surgery for recurrent CTS is more complex and has lower success rates (50-70%) than primary release.

faqs.question: Can I prevent carpal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes — occupational and ergonomic interventions significantly reduce CTS risk in high-risk occupations. Key prevention strategies: maintain neutral wrist posture during hand-intensive tasks (avoid sustained wrist flexion, extension, or deviation); take regular breaks (rotate tasks every 20-30 minutes to allow tendon sheaths to decompress); use ergonomic tools and keyboards that minimize grip force; use anti-vibration gloves when working with vibrating tools; maintain healthy body weight; treat underlying medical conditions (diabetes, hypothyroidism) optimally. These interventions are most effective when implemented before symptoms develop.

faqs.question: Is carpal tunnel syndrome genetic?
faqs.answer: There is no strong evidence for a direct genetic inheritance pattern for CTS. However, some anatomical factors that predispose to CTS — particularly a congenital small carpal tunnel relative to the nerve — are heritable. Having a first-degree relative with CTS is associated with a modest increase in risk, likely reflecting inherited differences in carpal tunnel anatomy. However, the most powerful risk factors for CTS are occupational (repetitive hand use, vibration exposure) and medical (diabetes, hypothyroidism, obesity), not genetic.

faqs.question: How long does carpal tunnel surgery take to heal?
faqs.answer: Recovery from carpal tunnel release is faster than most patients expect: within 24-48 hours — most patients note immediate relief of night pain and paresthesia. At 2-3 weeks — return to light activities (driving, light office work) is permitted; the bandage is removed and wound care begins. At 6-8 weeks — most patients have returned to full activities including grip-intensive tasks; grip strength typically returns to near-normal by 3 months. Nerve function (sensory and motor) continues to improve for 6-12 months post-surgery as the nerve remodels and regenerates. Some residual numbness may persist if there was significant pre-existing axonal damage.

faqs.question: What happens if carpal tunnel syndrome goes untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, CTS typically follows a chronic, progressive course. Approximately 50% of patients improve spontaneously within 10 months — the other 50% have persistent or progressive symptoms. Progressive untreated CTS leads to: worsening sensory loss (numbness becomes constant), thenar muscle weakness (grip and pinch strength decrease), and thenar muscle atrophy (visible shrinking of the thumb pad). If axonal damage has occurred for too long, the nerve damage may become irreversible — surgical decompression at this stage may not restore full function. Early treatment (splinting, injection) is more effective than waiting for severe nerve damage.

faqs.question: Will a wrist brace help carpal tunnel?
faqs.answer: Yes — a wrist splint (brace) in neutral position is the single most effective non-surgical treatment for CTS. The splint works by preventing wrist flexion and extension during sleep, which are the positions that increase carpal tunnel pressure most dramatically. Night splinting alone resolves symptoms in 30-40% of patients with mild CTS. For best results, the splint should hold the wrist in exactly neutral position (0 degrees) — splints that are too short or that hold the wrist in flexion actually worsen symptoms. Worn continuously for 4-6 weeks (not just at night) is more effective than intermittent use.

faqs.question: Is carpal tunnel syndrome a repetitive strain injury?
faqs.answer: Carpal tunnel syndrome is often classified as a repetitive strain injury (RSI) or work-related musculoskeletal disorder in occupational settings, but it is distinct from conditions like De Quervain's tenosynovitis in that it involves nerve compression rather than tendon inflammation. The relationship between occupational repetitive hand use and CTS is well-established — occupations with high repetition, high force, and vibration exposure carry the highest risk. However, not all CTS is occupational — many patients develop CTS without occupational risk factors (pregnancy, diabetes, hypothyroidism, congenital small carpal tunnel). The term 'RSI' encompasses many different conditions with different treatments.

faqs.question: Is carpal tunnel syndrome related to arthritis?
faqs.answer: Not directly — arthritis (osteoarthritis of the wrist) and CTS are different conditions, though they can coexist. Osteoarthritis of the wrist can contribute to CTS if bone spurs narrow the carpal tunnel. Inflammatory arthritis (rheumatoid arthritis) causes synovial inflammation that swells the flexor tendon sheaths within the carpal tunnel, increasing pressure on the median nerve — RA patients have significantly elevated CTS risk. When both conditions are present, treating the underlying arthritis is important for managing CTS effectively.

faqs.question: Does typing cause carpal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: The relationship between computer keyboard use and CTS is more complex than commonly believed. Prolonged typing itself has not been proven to cause CTS — the evidence is inconsistent. However, factors associated with computer work that DO increase CTS risk include: prolonged wrist flexion or deviation during typing, high grip force while using a mouse, and the sustained postures associated with poorly designed workstations. Proper ergonomic setup (keyboard at or below elbow height, wrists floating not resting, mouse sized to fit the hand, frequent task breaks) reduces CTS risk. Studies in data entry workers have shown variable CTS rates, suggesting that ergonomic factors matter more than typing per se.

metaTitle: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Surgery | aihealz
metaDescription: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes hand numbness, tingling, and weakness. Learn about wrist splints, corticosteroid injections, and carpal tunnel release surgery. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Diagram of the wrist showing the carpal tunnel and the median nerve passing through it
images.caption: The median nerve passes through the carpal tunnel — a narrow space between the carpal bones and the transverse carpal ligament.

images.altText: Diagram of the median nerve showing its sensory distribution in the hand (thumb, index, middle, half of ring finger)
images.caption: The median nerve provides sensation to the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and half of the ring finger — the characteristic distribution of carpal tunnel symptoms.

images.altText: Illustration showing carpal tunnel release surgery with division of the transverse carpal ligament
images.caption: Carpal tunnel release surgery divides the transverse carpal ligament, decompressing the median nerve.
